perturbation.original_text: 3.3 plan_b shall provide LICENSOR with a detailed written record, which includes the number of End User downloads and each distribution channel. Such report shall be provided to LICENSOR in electronic format within 6 weeks of the end of a quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 plan_b shall provide LICENSOR with a detailed written record, which includes the number of End User downloads and each distribution channel. Such report shall be provided to LICENSOR in electronic format within 16 weeks of the end of a quarter.
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the reporting deadline to 16 weeks after the quarter ends, potentially conflicting with standard accounting and audit requirements that necessitate more timely financial reporting. This delay could impede Licensor's ability to accurately assess royalties and revenues.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) emphasizes timely and accurate financial reporting. While this contract doesn't fall directly under SOX, excessively delaying financial reports (to 16 weeks) can hinder the ability of involved parties to meet regulatory standards regarding accurate and timely financial information, indirectly conflicting with the principles of SOX if either party is a publicly traded company. SOX requires companies to have internal controls in place to ensure the accuracy and reliability of financial reporting, and a 16-week delay could create challenges in meeting those requirements.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text extends the reporting deadline from 6 weeks to 16 weeks, which creates a potential contradiction with the principles of timely and accurate financial reporting, as indicated in the Law Explanation referencing the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX). While the contract doesn't directly fall under SOX, the extended delay could hinder compliance with regulations requiring accurate and timely financial information if either party is a publicly traded company. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7241, discusses corporate responsibility for financial reports and mentions companies filing periodic reports under section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title and certification requirements, suggesting an emphasis on accurate financial reporting. The increased reporting deadline weakens the original intent of timely reporting, aligning with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' where a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 Unless otherwise stated in the Appendix the term of this letter Agreement shall continue for twenty-four (24) months with the effective date unless terminated sooner or extended pursuant to the terms hereof ("Initial Term"). The Initial Term shall automatically be extended for an additional period of half a year unless either party provides the other party with written notification of termination of the letter Agreement at least 60 days prior to end of such period.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 Unless otherwise stated in the Appendix the term of this letter Agreement shall continue for twenty-four (24) months with the effective date unless terminated sooner or extended pursuant to the terms hereof ("Initial Term"). The Initial Term shall automatically be extended for an additional period of half a year unless either party provides the other party with written notification of termination of the letter Agreement at least 15 days prior to end of such period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 15 days may not provide sufficient time for either party to wind down operations or secure alternative arrangements, potentially conflicting with good faith and fair dealing requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification when an agreement is terminated. Reducing the notification period to 15 days might be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the established 24-month initial term and potential reliance interests built during that period. What is considered reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of such action, which includes the burden placed on the party to be notified. A 15 day notice may be viewed as too little time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 60 days to 15 days. UCC § 2-309(3) states that 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party'. The core of the legal issue is whether 15 days constitutes 'reasonable notification'. The law explanation highlights that a 15-day notice might be deemed unreasonable, especially given the 24-month initial term, creating a contradiction because the modified text potentially violates the 'reasonable notification' requirement mandated by UCC § 2-309(3).

perturbation.original_text: 3.4 LICENSOR shall have the right to use a certified public accountant to inspect and audit all the related records and books of plan_b to ensure plan_b's compliance with the terms of this Agreement. In the event that any such audit reveals that plan_b has underpaid fees to the value of ten (10) percent or more of the total amount of payments for the period covered by the audit, plan_b shall bear the cost of the audit and shall in any event immediately pay to LICENSOR Source: DATA CALL TECHNOLOGIES, SB-2/A, 9/18/2006 the full value of the underpaid or under-reported fees. Such audits shall normally be conducted during normal business hours at plan_b's premises.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4 LICENSOR shall have the right to use a certified public accountant to inspect and audit all the related records and books of plan_b to ensure plan_b's compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Audits can only occur outside of normal business hours at plan_b's premises. In the event that any such audit reveals that plan_b has underpaid fees to the value of fifty (50) percent or more of the total amount of payments for the period covered by the audit, plan_b shall bear the cost of the audit and shall in any event immediately pay to LICENSOR Source: DATA CALL TECHNOLOGIES, SB-2/A, 9/18/2006 the full value of the underpaid or under-reported fees.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting audits to outside normal business hours and raising the underpayment threshold to 50% before plan_b bears the audit cost could be deemed an unreasonable limitation on the licensor's audit rights, conflicting with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of a contract. Restricting audits to outside business hours and significantly raising the underpayment threshold could be seen as impeding the licensor's ability to reasonably verify compliance, potentially violating this implied covenant. By making it harder for Licensor to conduct an effective audit and recover costs, plan_b may be hindering the purpose of the original agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an inconsistency that contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract allows for audits during normal business hours, providing reasonable access to LICENSOR. The changed text limits audits to outside business hours and significantly raises the underpayment threshold to 50%, making it more difficult for LICENSOR to verify compliance and recover audit costs. This impedes LICENSOR's ability to reasonably verify compliance, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that good faith encompasses 'observance of fair dealing standards'. The modification here arguably violates fair dealing standards because it unreasonably restricts the licensor's right to audit.
